title: Ho Feng-Shan: The Chinese Diplomat Who Issued Visas to Jews
title_zh: 何凤山：向犹太人发放签证的中国外交官
title_he: הו פנג-שאן: הדיפלומט הסיני שהנפיק אשרות ליהודים
description: The story of China's consul in Vienna who issued thousands of visas to Shanghai, enabling Jews to escape Nazi-occupied Austria.
author: Torah Light Team

historical_period.chinese_equivalent: Second Sino-Japanese War / 抗日战争时期 (1937-1945)

people_mentioned.name: Ho Feng-Shan
people_mentioned.significance: Issued thousands of visas to Shanghai that enabled Austrian Jews to escape Nazi persecution

people_mentioned.name: Chen Jie
people_mentioned.role: Chinese Ambassador to Germany (based in Berlin)
people_mentioned.significance: Ho's superior who ordered him to stop issuing visas

people_mentioned.name: Eric Goldstaub
people_mentioned.role: Jewish refugee saved by Ho's visa
people_mentioned.significance: Later discovered Ho was still alive and helped bring his story to light

people_mentioned.name: Manli Ho
people_mentioned.significance: Received the Righteous Among the Nations award on behalf of her father

key_concepts.definition: Highest honor Israel bestows on non-Jews who risked their lives to save Jews during the Holocaust
Confucian virtue representing the moral obligation to do what is right even when difficult or costly
Nazi Germany's annexation of Austria in March 1938

cultural_parallels: Confucian concept of Yi (义) and Jewish concept of moral obligation
Chinese and Jewish responses to rescue and gratitude (Hakarat Hatov)
Shanghai as refuge during shared suffering of both peoples
Parallel with Japanese diplomat Sugihara Chiune
header_image_prompt: A dramatic Chinese brushstroke landscape painting in traditional 山水 style depicting Vienna's skyline with the distinctive Stephansdom cathedral in the background, transitioning into a stylized representation of Shanghai's waterfront. In the foreground, a figure in 1930s-era diplomatic dress stands at a desk with stacks of visa documents, his posture conveying quiet determination. A shaft of light breaks through storm clouds above, illuminating the visa papers. The color palette features muted grays and blues for the troubled European scene, gradually warming to golden earth tones representing hope and Shanghai as sanctuary. Style: ink wash with subtle sepia accents, contemplative yet urgent mood, emphasizing the bridge between two worlds through one man's moral courage.
notes: Article includes detailed historical context, cultural parallels between Confucian and Jewish values, practical application for readers, and comprehensive bibliography with verified URLs. All three language versions completed with consistent content.
